pattern: Only the current positions of the smallest and largest values matter -- every adjacent swap that isn't moving one of them toward its target is wasted, and the two journeys only interfere when the smallest value currently sits to the right of the largest value.
hints: You never need to touch any value other than the smallest and the largest -- everything else can stay wherever it lands.
Find the current index of the smallest value and the current index of the largest value; each needs enough adjacent swaps to reach its target end proportional to its distance from that end.
Add the two distances together, then subtract one extra swap if the smallest value currently sits to the right of the largest value, since one swap between them helps both at once.

edge_inputs.description: smallest before largest, small gap to close
smallest after largest, one shared swap helps both
already in final position, no swaps needed
middle elements scrambled but ends already correct, answer stays zero
smallest sits immediately after largest, shared swap applies
smallest and largest at opposite extreme ends, no shared swap

stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 50))
    nums = list(range(1, n + 1))
    rng.shuffle(nums)
    return [nums]

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def semiOrderedPermutation(self, nums) -> int:
        n = len(nums)
        target = list(range(1, n + 1))
        cur = list(nums)
        swaps = 0
        while cur[0] != 1:
            i = cur.index(1)
            cur[i - 1], cur[i] = cur[i], cur[i - 1]
            swaps += 1
        while cur[-1] != n:
            i = cur.index(n)
            cur[i], cur[i + 1] = cur[i + 1], cur[i]
            swaps += 1
        return swaps
